The quadratic equation for the batting score is \( S = 50 + 5n - 0.2n^2 \).
To find the maximum score, we locate the vertex of this parabola.
The vertex occurs at \( n = -b / (2a) \) using standard form \( ax^2 + bx + c \).
Here \( a = -0.2 \) and \( b = 5 \).
Substituting values gives \( n = -5 / (2 \times -0.2) \).
This simplifies to \( n = -5 / -0.4 \).
The result is \( n = 12.5 \).
Since practice sessions must be whole numbers, we test integers 12 and 13.
For \( n = 12 \), the score is \( 50 + 60 - 28.8 = 81.2 \).
For \( n = 13 \), the score is \( 50 + 65 - 33.8 = 81.2 \).
Both values yield the same maximum average batting score of 81.2.
Thus, the optimal number of sessions is either 12 or 13.
Now we calculate the win probability for the second sub-problem.
The formula is \( P = 1 / (1 + e^{-(0.1S - 3)}) \).
First, find \( S \) when \( n = 5 \).
\( S_5 = 50 + 25 - 0.2(25) = 65 \).
Next, find \( S \) when \( n = 10 \).
\( S_{10} = 50 + 50 - 0.2(100) = 80 \).
Calculate probability \( P_5 \) using \( S_5 = 65 \).
The exponent becomes \( -(0.1 \times 65 - 3) = -3.5 \).
\( P_5 = 1 / (1 + e^{-3.5}) \approx 0.9705 \).
